coredata.dc:title: Microwave assisted synthesis of boron and nitrogen rich graphitic quantum dots to enhance fluorescence of photosynthetic pigments

Aykut, Sümeyye

Ünlü, Caner
coredata.dc:description: Energy transfer between quantum dots and biomolecules is of interest due to high absorption capacity and high quantum efficiency of quantum dots. Amongst all types of quantum dots, graphene and graphitic quantum dots have great potential for energy transfer studies due to their high biocompatibility and low toxicity. In this study, a simple route to synthesize boron and nitrogen rich graphitic quantum dots (C-BN) and boron carbon nitride (BCN) quantum dots is demonstrated. Quantum dots were synthesized in a domestic microwave oven. Composition of quantum dots was controlled by tuning initial mole ratio of boron and nitrogen precursors. As molar ratio of boron precursor was increased, formation of C-BN quantum dots was favoured. C-BN quantum dots were mainly composed of boron and nitrogen (with around 10 % carbon) in their main composition, and sized around 2 nm with bright photoluminescence. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study which proposes a bottom-up synthesis method to synthesize C-BN quantum dots based on domestic microwaves. Also, treating photosynthetic pigments with quantum dots resulted in 20 % enhancement of fluorescence of photosynthetic pigments at 670 nm, which demonstrates that C-BN and BCN quantum dots can be important constituents of artificial antenna systems for photosynthetic organisms.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Synthesis of C-BN QDs and BCN QDs in a domestic microwave. By changing mass ratio of Boric acid or Urea (with trace amount of citric acid), the composition and structure of QD can be controlled. C-BN and BCN QDs were highly luminescent under UV light (λexc=366 nm).
a) XPS survey of BCN and C-BN QDs. b) High resolution XPS survey of B 1 s peak of BCN and C-BN QDs (see Table 1 for abbreviations of QDs).
TEM image of C-BN QDs. For C-BN QDs, C-BN-1 is analysed. (see Table 1).
TEM image of BCN QDs. For BCN QDs, BCN-1 is analysed. (see Table 1).
Size distribution of C-BN and BCN QDs. For C-BN QDs, C-BN-1 is analysed. For BCN QDs, BCN-1 is analysed (see Table 1).
FTIR spectra of C-BN and BCN QDs. For C-BN QDs, C-BN-1 is analysed. For BCN QDs, BCN-1 is analysed (see Table 1).
Emission spectra of graphene, CN and BCN quantum dots dissolved in ultrapure water. All emission spectra are collected with λexc =350 nm and normalized to 1 at highest peak point.

a) Absorption and emission spectra of C-BN-1 and BCN-1 QDs dissolved in ultrapure water. In both spectra, emission and absorption maximum shifted towards lower wavelengths as structure shifts from BCN to C-BN. b) Emission spectra of C-BN-1, C-BN-2, BCN-1 and BCN-2 QDs. The emission wavelength shifted towards lower wavelengths as boron amount in QDs increased (see Table 1).

a) Emission spectrum of photosynthetic pigments (PP) and photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures. The spectrum of all solutions were identical, without any emission peak which rises due to existence of QDs. b) Excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigments (PP) and photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures at emission wavelength of 673 nm. The excitation spectrum become enhanced at lower wavelengths as QDs added to PP solutions. c) Excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigments (PP) and photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures at emission wavelength of 673 nm at lower wavelengths. Energy transfer from QDs to PP is more efficient for C-BN QDs at 350 nm – 375 nm region compared to BCN QDs.
Optical properties of C-BN and BCN quantum dots.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Energy transfer between quantum dots and biomolecules is of interest due to high absorption capacity and high quantum efficiency of quantum dots. Amongst all types of quantum dots, graphene and graphitic quantum dots have great potential for energy transfer studies due to their high biocompatibility and low toxicity. In this study, a simple route to synthesize boron and nitrogen rich graphitic quantum dots (C-BN) and boron carbon nitride (BCN) quantum dots is demonstrated. Quantum dots were synthesized in a domestic microwave oven. Composition of quantum dots was controlled by tuning initial mole ratio of boron and nitrogen precursors. As molar ratio of boron precursor was increased, formation of C-BN quantum dots was favoured. C-BN quantum dots were mainly composed of boron and nitrogen (with around 10 % carbon) in their main composition, and sized around 2 nm with bright photoluminescence. To the best of our knowledge, this is the first study which proposes a bottom-up synthesis method to synthesize C-BN quantum dots based on domestic microwaves. Also, treating photosynthetic pigments with quantum dots resulted in 20 % enhancement of fluorescence of photosynthetic pigments at 670 nm, which demonstrates that C-BN and BCN quantum dots can be important constituents of artificial antenna systems for photosynthetic organisms.

ce:section.ce:para: Luminescent semiconductor nanocrystals with smaller radius than bohr exciton radius, in other words quantum dots (QDs), are essential nanostructures with unique photophysical properties, such as high quantum yield, high photostability, size controlled emission characteristics, etc… and frequently used in biotechnology [1–7]. However, most of the quantum dots contain toxic-heavy metals (generally cadmium) [5–10] and toxic effects of quantum dots limit their application fields in biotechnology. Nowadays, toxic effects of cadmium based quantum dots can be overcome by using cadmium-free quantum dots, especially by using 2-D quantum dots; quantum dots with size confinement at 2 dimensions [11–13].

Graphene quantum dots are the most famous 2-D quantum dots and frequently studied in literature [13–19]. Graphene quantum dots can be synthesized by various bottom-up synthesis methods and can be produced easily by using inexpensive domestic microwaves [20–25]. Graphene quantum dots which are synthesized in domestic microwaves have high product yield, high quantum yield and obtained within minutes [20–25]. Also, different atoms, such as nitrogen, boron, sulphur, etc…, can be doped in graphene quantum dots by using domestic microwaves and by doping heteroatoms in graphene quantum dots, photophysical properties of graphene quantum dots can be enhanced [20–26]. However, exact control on optical properties of graphene quantum dots is still a challenge and requires extra experimental steps, which makes synthesis and use of graphene quantum dots unpractical for many applications [13–19].

Alternatively, C3N4 graphitic quantum dots have become center of attention with their excellent features such as high photostability, high quantum yield, low toxicity, easy preparation and good biocompatibility [20,21,27–33]. C3N4 graphitic quantum dots are used in various sensor studies [20,32,34,35] and their application field is enhanced by doping C3N4 graphitic quantum dots with different atoms such as oxygen, phosphorus or sulphur [27,33,36]. However, as was mentioned in graphene quantum dots, exact control on optical properties of graphitic C3N4 quantum dots is a challenge, too [20,21,27–33].

In addition to C3N4 graphitic quantum dots, in last 5 years hexagonal boron nitride (BN) quantum dots have attracted attention of researchers with their excellent biocompatibility and low toxicity [37–45]. Boron nitride quantum dots were first synthesized at 2014 [37]. To date, boron nitride quantum dots are frequently synthesized by top-down synthesis methods [37,39,40,42,44–48] and bottom-up synthesis methods are rarely used to synthesize boron nitride quantum dots [43,47,49,50]. Also, bottom-up synthesis methods for BN quantum dots require high temperature, high pressure and long synthesis duration [43,47,49,50].

Prior to their excellent photophysical properties, good biocompatibility and low toxicity, 2-D quantum dots are potential candidates as energy donors in energy transfer studies through biomolecules [51–56]. To date, graphene quantum dots have been studied extensively as biosensors, whose main working principle depends on Förster resonance energy transfer (FRET) [17,57–64]. Also, there are numerous studies on energy transfer between phthalocyanines, a chlorophyll like molecule, and quantum dots [65–70]. However, despite photosynthetic pigments have similar chemical structures with phthalocyanines, until now there are only few studies on energy transfer from quantum dots to photosynthetic pigments, in which only graphene quantum dots are used [71,72].

In our study, boron and nitrogen rich graphitic quantum dots (C-BN) and boron carbon nitride (BCN) quantum dots (QDs) were synthesized by using a domestic microwave at one step, under ambient conditions within 5 min. C-BN and BCN quantum dots were highly luminescent with size around 2 nm. Composition of C-BN and BCN quantum dots was controlled by controlling initial amounts of boron and nitrogen precursors. As a consequence, optical properties of C-BN and BCN quantum dots was controlled by controlling composition of quantum dots. Amount of carbon precursor was kept very low in order to decrease amount of carbon in quantum dot structure. This is the first study that used bottom-up synthesis method in which inexpensive domestic microwaves were used to synthesize C-BN quantum dots. Also, energy transfer between photosynthetic pigments and C-BN, BCN quantum dots were observed by using fluorescence spectroscopy. Our results revealed that C-BN and BCN quantum dots can be used to enhance absorption and fluorescence capacity of photosynthetic pigments.
All chemicals used in this work were of the highest purity and were purchased from the Sigma- Aldrich Co. They were used without further purification.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of C-BN and BCN QDs
ce:section.ce:para: In a typical synthesis procedure, 0.1 mg citric acid (carbon precursor), 100 mg urea (nitrogen precursor) and 100 mg boric acid (boron precursor) were dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and added into a 50 ml reaction flask with flat bottom. Then, the clear solution was placed in a domestic microwave (Brand: Arçelik MD554) and heated at 750 MW on a rotating plate for 5 min. Solvent of the solution was evaporated within 2 min and the remaining solid mixture was kept in microwave for an additional 3 min. The crude solid product was yellowish for aforementioned citric acid, boric acid and urea amounts, but when amount of urea was increased, the crude solid product became yellower and when amount of boric acid was increased, the crude solid product became whiter. The crude solid product was then dissolved in 10 ml ultrapure water and first filtrated through a qualitative filter paper with 10 μm pore size, then was filtered through a special filter paper with 0.22 μm pore size. This procedure was repeated twice. Afterwards, quantum dots were centrifuged at 14,000 rpm for half an hour. Finally, quantum dots were precipitated by adding methanol:toluene 50/50 v/v to the solution. Then, the solvent of the solution was removed and the purified solid product was kept for further analysis. Each purification step was repeated at least twice. Urea and boric acid amounts were changed in order to obtain quantum dots with different compositions.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical and structural characterization of C-BN and BCN QDs
ce:section.ce:para: To study optical properties of C-BN and BCN QDs, absorption spectra of QDs were recorded by Scinco Neosys-2000 double- beam ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) spectrophotometer. Emission spectra of quantum dots were recorded by Varian Cary Eclipse fluorescence spectrofluorimeter. Emission spectrum of each sample was collected by using excitation wavelength (λexc) 350 nm. Each sample was dissolved in 10 ml of ultrapure water after purification and diluted until optical density was under 0.1 in order to prevent errors resulting from self-absorption. The emission colour of each sample was observed under 366 nm UV-light. Quantum yields of the C-BN and BCN QDs were measured by using coumarine 102 in ethanol as a standard (the reference quantum yield was 0.93) [73,74]. All optical characterizations were performed on aqueous solutions of QDs.

For structural analysis, X-Ray Photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS), fouirer transform infrared spectroscopy (FTIR), Transmission electron microscopy (TEM) and dynamic light scattering (DLS) were used. Chemical composition of C-BN and BCN quantum dots were determined by XPS. XPS spectra of purified quantum dots were collected by Thermo Scientific K-Alpha with a monochromatic 1486.68 eV Al Kα X-ray line source and a 400 μm beam size.

In order to understand bonding characterization of C-BN and BCN quantum dots, quantum dots were characterized by attenuated total reflectance FTIR spectroscopy. FTIR spectrum of each purified quantum dot was recorded in the range of 3750–800 cm−1 by using a Perkin Elmer ATR-FTIR spectrophotometer.

Shape and size of quantum dots were determined by using TEM. TEM measurements were carried out with JEOL JEM 1220 at an accelerate voltage of 100 kV. Also, TEM measurements were supported by DLS measurements. Purified QDs were precipitated and re-dissolved in N,N-dimethylformamide (DMF) in order to prevent agglomeration. Then size distribution of QDs in DMF was obtained by using DLS (Malvern Nanosizer).

ce:section.ce:section-title: Photosynthetic pigment isolation and optical characterization of photosynthetic pigment – quantum dot mixture
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthetic pigments were isolated from spinach according to the literature with following procedure [75]. 2 g of fresh spinach leaves were washed by water and then dried. After that, spinach leaves were cut into small pieces and transferred into a mortar. Then, 10 ml methanol was added into the mortar. Leaves were grind up with pestle until methanol became dark green color. The solution was filtered by using filter paper for separation of pellets. Quality of photosynthetic pigments were controlled by their emission under UV-light and emission spectrum of photosynthetic pigments [76,77].

Isolated photosynthetic pigments and quantum dots were stirred in DMF in order to create an interaction between photosynthetic pigments and quantum dots. 50 mg dried photosynthetic pigments were dissolved in 1 ml DMF and mixed with 50 mg of QDs dissolved in 1 ml DMF. Photosynthetic pigment – QD solution was stirred for 24 h at room temperature. Then, mixtures were diluted by 1:20 ratio with DMF for fluorescence analysis.

Energy transfer between photosynthetic pigments and QDs was observed by collecting emission/excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigments and photosynthetic pigment – QDs mixtures. Emission spectrum of each sample was collected by using excitation wavelength (λexc) 350 nm. The emission spectrum of each photosynthetic pigment – QD mixture was compared with emission spectrum of pure photosynthetic pigment to observe differences between emission characteristics of photosynthetic pigments and emission characteristics of photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures. Energy transfer between photosynthetic pigments and QDs was observed by comparing excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigments and photosynthetic pigments – QDs mixture. Excitation spectrum of each solution was recorded at emission wavelength of 673 nm (The first peak at emission spectrum of photosynthetic pigments) and normalized at 615 nm, where quantum dots have neither emission nor absorption.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Microwave synthesis of C-BN and BCN quantum dots
Structural characterization of C-BN and BCN quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: The chemical composition of C-BN and BCN quantum dots were determined by XPS. Previously, XPS analysis on B doped graphene quantum dots showed that atomic composition of B doped carbon quantum dots was 8.2 % by nitrogen, 65 % by carbon and 2.4 % by boron and 22.9 % by oxygen [23]. In our study, as the mass ratio of boric acid: urea: citric acid was set to 1000:1000:1, B:C:N:O atomic ratio for quantum dots observed to be 14:38:18:35 (Fig. 2). This result indicated that as citric acid mass ratio was decreased in synthesis medium, atomic ratio of carbon in quantum dot structure decreased as well where atomic ratio of boron and nitrogen increased accordingly, and BCN quantum dots formed instead of B doped carbon quantum dots. However, it should be noted that when citric acid mass ratio is decreased below 1 where mass ratio of boric acid and urea were kept at 1000 (or over) in synthesis medium, the final product did not emit light upon UV light excitation and therefore it was concluded that quantum dot formation was not observed. Also, BCN quantum dots were synthesized by using boric acid: urea : citric acid mass ratio as 1000 : 3000 : 1 in synthesis medium (Fig. 2). In this case, B:C:N:O atomic ratio found to be 13:34:17:35 (Fig. 2). When boric acid : urea : citric acid mass ratio was set to 3000:1000:1 in synthesis medium, B:C:N:O atomic ratio changed to 22:12:21:45, atomic ratio of carbon in quantum dot structure decreased significantly and this observation showed formation of C-BN quantum dots (Fig. 2). This result revealed that as the relative mass ratio of boric acid increased in synthesis medium, formation of C-BN quantum dots was propagated. However, as the boric acid: urea : citric acid mass ratio was changed to 4000 : 1000 : 1 in synthesis medium, brightness of quantum dots decreased significantly (see decrease in quantum yield in Table 1 ). Also, it should be noted that oxygen exists in carboxylic and hydroxyl groups which exist on the surface of graphene, graphitic and boron nitride quantum dots [13,16,17,41,43,49,78], therefore oxygen appears in elemental analysis of BCN and C-BN quantum dots. High resolution XPS analysis on B 1 s peak of quantum dots showed that B 1 s peak shifts to lower bonding energies in XPS survey of C-BN quantum dots (Fig. 2). Since BN bonding appears at lower eV values (190.8 eV) [50] compared to BC and BO bondings (193.3 eV and 191.4 eV, respectively, respectively) [50] in XPS analysis, this result showed that as boron and nitrogen amount increases in quantum dot structure, BN bonding becomes dominant and quantum dot structure become more like boron nitride quantum dots (Fig. 2). As an overall conclusion, chemical composition of C-BN and BCN quantum dots can be controlled by tuning the initial molar ratio of boric acid, urea and citric acid. Further structural analysis were focused on C-BN quantum dots synthesized with Boric acid : Urea : Citric acid mass ratio 3000 : 1000 : 1 and BCN quantum dots synthesized with Boric acid : Urea : Citric acid ratio 1000 : 3000 : 1 due to their higher quantum yield compared to other C-BN and BCN quantum dots respectively (See Table 1) and these quantum dots were further used for energy transfer studies between photosynthetic pigments and quantum dots (due to their higher quantum yields) (Table 1).

In addition, FTIR measurements were conducted to have a detailed information about bonding character and structure of C-BN and BCN quantum dots. In FTIR spectra of C-BN and BCN quantum dots, the vibration bands around 1585 cm−1, 1630 cm−1 and 1685 cm−1 were ascribed to (C)-(BN) bonding vibrations [37,44]. The intensity of (C)(BN) vibration band was significantly low in infrared spectrum of C-BN quantum dots, where this band appeared with considerably high intensity in FTIR spectrum of BCN quantum dots (Fig. 6 ). The comparison of FTIR spectrum of all synthesized quantum dots in this study revealed the same observation, as amount of urea increases, composition of quantum dots became more carbon rich (See supplementary information, Figure S1). This observation suggests that, in both C-BN and BCN quantum dots carbon appears to be in structure, however, the amount of carbon significantly decreases in C-BN quantum dots. The same conclusion can be achieved by comparing the peak around 1070 cm-1 in FTIR spectra of C-BN and BCN quantum dots, which is ascribed to CB bending vibration (Fig. 6) [83,84]. In BCN quantum dots, CB bending vibration peak appeared with a high intensity, however, in CBN quantum dots this peak appeared as a weak shoulder around 1080 cm−1 with a considerably low intensity (Fig. 6). In FTIR spectra of both BCN quantum dots and C-BN quantum dots, peaks around 1330 cm−1 - 1380 cm−1 were ascribed to BN stretching vibrations, which indicated formation of BN structure (Fig. 6) [37,44,85]. Also, the peak around 920 cm−1 was ascribed to BNO bonding vibration, this peak had high intensity in FTIR spectrum of BCN quantum dots, which either indicated that oxygen existed in the BCN quantum dot structure or BCN quantum dots had BNO edges (Fig. 6) [49,85]. However, the intensity of 920 cm−1 decreased significantly in CBN quantum dots, where the intensity of 1190 cm−1 increased drastically, which corresponds to BOH bending vibration (Fig. 5) [44,83–85]. The intensity of 1190 cm−1 peak was relatively low in FTIR spectrum of BCN quantum dots (Fig. 6). This observation concluded that as the structure shifts from BCN to CBN, the amount of BNO bonding in quantum dot structure decreased and amount of BOH increased correspondingly. Therefore, this observation suggested that the surface of CBN quantum dots were mostly covered with hydroxyl groups which were attached to boron, and the existence of BNO bonding decreased in CBN quantum dots which meant that oxygen is not a part of core nanocrystal structure (or exist in nanocrystal structure with trace amount), because if it were, we would expect a similar contribution from BNO vibration band in infrared spectrum of CBN quantum dots, but rather a dramatic decrease in intensity of 920 cm−1 peak was observed where the peak around 1190 cm−1 become more intense (Fig. 6). The broad peak around 3000 – 3500 cm−1 with relatively high intensity was attributed to OH and NH bondings due to fact that the surface of CBN and BCN quantum dots were mostly covered by OH and NH (Fig. 6) [44,83–85].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Optical characterization of CBN and BCN quantum dots
ce:section.ce:para: Optical characterization of C-BN and BCN quantum dots were carried out by using fluorescence and UV-VIS spectrometers. Emission characteristics of BCN and C-BN quantum dots showed significant differences when they were compared to emission characteristics of graphene quantum dots (synthesized by using citric acid only) and CN quantum dots (synthesized by using urea and citric acid with urea: citric acid mass ratio 1000 : 1) which were synthesized under same conditions as C-BN and BCN quantum dots were synthesized, but without adding boron precursor (Fig. 7 ). Two important outcomes with these control experiments were achieved; 1) In the absence of boric acid, CN quantum dots did not show a uniform distribution either in size or in composition whose emission spectrum had 2 emission peaks (Fig. 7). 2) Compared to graphene quantum dots, the emission maximum shifted to lower wavelengths in BCN quantum dots (Fig. 7). These observations stated that boron became a main constituent of quantum dot structure and affected stability and emission behaviour of quantum dots. It should be noted that in the absence of urea, treating boric acid – citric acid in microwave did not yield any kind of material with fluorescence.

Comparison of absorption spectra of C-BN and BCN quantum dots showed that the absorption maximum shifts from 335 nm to 295 nm as the boron amount increased in quantum dot structure (Fig. 8 ). Similar characteristic was observed in emission spectra as well (Fig. 8). The emission peak of quantum dots shifted from 455 nm to 435 nm as the boron amount in nanocrystal increased (Fig. 8). BCN quantum dots were emitting bright light with quantum yield around 30 %, however, quantum yield of C-BN quantum dots decreased to 15 % and as the boron amount in nanocrystal increased, the quantum yield of C-BN quantum dots decreased further (Table 1).

It should be noted that microwave synthesis method is not a feasible method to control the optical parameters of quantum dot [21–23,86]. Because, generally optical parameters of graphene quantum dots can be controlled by tuning size of quantum dot and the size control with microwave synthesis is almost impossible [21–23,86]. On the other hand, for ternary quantum dots, either alloyed or doped, it is possible to control optical parameters of quantum dots by tuning composition of quantum dots [87,88]. In our study, emission and absorption parameters of quantum dots were controlled by changing molar ratio of boron and nitrogen precursor to tune composition of C-BN and BCN quantum dots and therefore an exact control over optical and structural parameters of C-BN and BCN quantum dots was provided by using a simple, domestic microwave.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fluorescence enhancement in photosynthetic pigment – quantum dot mixture
ce:section.ce:para: Photosynthetic pigments mainly absorb light between 350 nm – 550 nm and 620 nm–700 nm with several absorption peaks, and emit light around 670 nm and 715 nm upon excitation (λexc) with 350 nm [77,89,90]. However, the amount of light absorbed by photosynthetic pigments in spectrum region between 350 nm and 400 nm is limited. Therefore, whole light spectrum cannot be used efficiently by photosynthetic pigments in photosynthesis [77,89,90]. In our study, photosynthetic pigments isolated from spinach were mixed with C-BN and BCN quantum dots to enhance fluorescence capacity of photosynthetic pigments in spectrum range between 350 nm and 425 nm by achieving energy transfer from QDs to photosynthetic pigments. The emission spectrum of pure photosynthetic pigments and emission spectra of photosynthetic pigments – QDs mixtures were identical, a dominant emission peak around 673 nm and a shoulder around 715 nm with λexc =350 nm, which are the characteristic emission peaks for photosynthetic pigments (Fig. 9 a) [77,89,90]. This observation revealed two important conclusions; ;1) Emission spectrum of photosynthetic pigments did not change in the presence of quantum dots, 2) Emission of quantum dots completely quenched in the presence of photosynthetic pigments because emission peak of BCN and C-BN QDs was not observed in photosynthetic pigment – QD mixture with λexc =350 nm. Two possible scenarios may explain quenching of QD emission, either QDs are decomposed or the emission of QDs was disappeared due to energy transfer from QDs to photosynthetic pigments.

In order to understand the reason behind quenching of fluorescence of QDs in the presence of photosynthetic pigments, excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigments and excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigment-QDs mixtures were collected at the main emission peak of photosynthetic pigments (673 nm) and compared. Excitation spectra of QD – photosynthetic pigments and photosynthetic pigments were normalized at 615 nm to have a meaningful comparison. Both QDs and photosynthetic pigments can be excited in the spectrum range of 350 nm–435 nm. So, the emission at 673 nm by exciting photosynthetic pigment – QD mixture with λexc =350 nm – 435 nm may arise due to two factors; 1) excitation of photosynthetic pigments, 2) energy transfer from QDs to photosynthetic pigments since both quantum dots and photosynthetic pigments absorb light between 350 nm – 435 nm. On the other hand, when photosynthetic pigment-QDs mixture is excited by λexc =615 nm (the wavelength which is used for normalization), only photosynthetic pigments are excited since C-BN and BN QDs do not absorb any light around 615 nm (Fig. 8) and all the emission that appears at 673 nm must be due to excitation of photosynthetic pigments. In parallel with this, photosynthetic pigment solution without any QD is considered as a reference, since emission at 673 nm either by λexc = 350−435 nm or by λexc =615 nm must be due to excitation of pure photosynthetic pigments. In light of these information, in order to observe the effect of energy transfer from QDs to photosynthetic pigments, excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigments and photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures were recorded at emission wavelength of 673 nm and all excitation spectra were normalized to 615 nm. The increase in relative intensity of excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigment-QD mixture at 350 nm – 425 nm range compared to excitation spectrum of pure photosynthetic pigments is evidence of energy transfer from QDs to photosynthetic pigments. Despite the excitation spectra of all samples looked like similar, the relative intensity of the peak around 411 nm increased significantly (by 20 %) in excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigment-QDs mixtures (Fig. 9b). If QDs were decomposed and no energy was transferred to photosynthetic pigments, then the excitation spectrum of pure photosynthetic pigments and the excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigments-QDs mixtures should be identical at all points, however, the increase around 411 nm peak at excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigment-QDs mixture suggested that there was an energy transfer between QDs and photosynthetic pigments (Fig. 9b). Our observations revealed that the energy transfer from C-BN QDs to photosynthetic pigments was more efficient than the energy transfer from BCN QDs to photosynthetic pigments between 350 nm and 375 nm spectrum range (Fig. 9c). The excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigment – C-BN QDs enhanced significantly (around 20 %) between 350 nm and 375 nm. However, the excitation spectrum of photosynthetic pigment – BCN QDs was similar with excitation spectrum of pure photosynthetic pigments between 350 nm and 375 nm, which suggested that energy transfer was not efficient between BCD QDs and photosynthetic pigments at that spectrum range (Fig. 9c). On the other hand, between 375 nm and 425 nm, both C-BN and BCN QDs transferred energy efficiently to photosynthetic pigments (Fig. 9c). In conclusion, the excitation spectra of photosynthetic pigment – QD mixtures revealed that a significant amount of energy was transferred from QDs to photosynthetic pigments between 350 nm and 425 nm. Further detailed NMR and time-resolved fluorescence spectroscopy studies should be performed in order to explain the nature of energy transfer in details.

Boron and nitrogen rich graphitic and boron carbon nitride quantum dots with bright emission were synthesized by a simple domestic microwave system. Boric acid and urea with a trace amount of citric acid were used as boron, nitrogen and carbon precursors, respectively. C-BN and BCN QDs were synthesized within 5 min, with uniform size distribution (around 2 nm) and with high quantum yield between 15 % and 30 %. The composition and therefore optical properties of QDs were controlled by tuning molar ratio of boron and nitrogen precursors. Finally, to check energy transfer between quantum dots and photosynthetic pigments, C-BN and BCN quantum dots were dissolved in DMF and mixed with photosynthetic pigments in DMF. The change in excitation and emission spectra of quantum dot – photosynthetic pigment mixture compared to excitation and emission spectra of pure quantum dots and pure photosynthetic pigments indicated that energy transfer occur from quantum dots to photosynthetic pigments.
